metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact CLI invocation to pause the reconciler schedule (e.g., `gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml`) AND address the RECONCILE_CONTINUOUS self-rescheduling chain interaction (if the chain is active, a schedule disable is insufficient; document whether `gh run cancel` or flipping the Variable off is also required); include a concrete verification command to confirm quiescence before starting repairs.
findings.finding: The plan names 'disable the `reconcile-bridge.yml` GHA schedule' as the PRIMARY safety mechanism (reconciler pause) before a destructive 2422-ticket live-store repair, but never specifies the concrete tool+subcommand required. The GH CLI `gh workflow disable` / `gh workflow enable` sub-commands are the actual mechanism, but they are Unverified at flag level in this environment and absent from the plan body. An executor could use the wrong method (e.g., commenting out the `schedule:` stanza and pushing, or using the GitHub web UI), and the ops doc is only promised to be 'captured in the PR description / a linked ops doc' — not specified in the plan itself.
findings.location: Safety section — 'Reconciler pause (real mechanism)'; AC3 — 'Reconciler paused by disabling the GHA schedule'; Round-5 refinements — 'rollback procedure + reconciler-pause steps captured in the PR description / a linked ops doc'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ops doc (and plan AC3): `gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml` to stop cron-triggered runs; if `RECONCILE_CONTINUOUS=true`, also set the repo Variable to `false` (or cancel any queued dispatch run via `gh run cancel`) before starting. Verify with `gh workflow view reconcile-bridge.yml --json state` (state == 'disabled') and `gh run list --workflow=reconcile-bridge.yml --status=in_progress --limit=1` (empty). Re-enable with `gh workflow enable reconcile-bridge.yml` after completion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `_commands/compact.py (shared retire helper)` from the Scope section to match Round-5's explicit 'removed from Scope' declaration.
findings.finding: The Scope section still lists `_commands/compact.py (shared retire helper)` as a file this ticket edits, but Round-5 refinements explicitly state 'this ticket does NOT edit `compact.py` (removed from Scope)'. An implementer reading Scope would conclude compact.py is in scope; reading Round-5 would conclude the opposite.
findings.location: Scope section (last paragraph) vs Round-5 refinements (HELPER OWNERSHIP bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the `, _commands/compact.py (shared retire helper)` clause from the Scope section, leaving only `_commands/fsck.py` and its parenthetical.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `_commands/compact.py` from the Scope section (Round-5 already decided this is A1-owned); confirm Scope lists only `_commands/fsck.py` as the A3 edit target.
findings.finding: The Scope section still names `_commands/compact.py (shared retire helper)` as an A3 edit target, but Round-5 refinements explicitly state 'this ticket does NOT edit `compact.py` (removed from Scope) — it calls A1's helper.' The stale entry in Scope directly contradicts Round-5, leaving an implementer who reads Scope first with a wrong edit target.
findings.location: Scope section — '`_commands/compact.py` (shared retire helper)' and Round-5 refinements — 'HELPER OWNERSHIP'
findings.suggested_fix: Delete `_commands/compact.py (shared retire helper)` from the Scope file list, leaving only `_commands/fsck.py (--repair/--dry-run/--limit + per-batch git commit + local marker + atomic per-snapshot rewrite + retire-still-present)`.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a safety guard for the GHA schedule re-enable: either (a) implement a try/finally wrapper in the remediation script that calls the GitHub API to re-enable the schedule on any exit, or (b) set a calendar/monitoring alert that fires if the schedule has been disabled for more than N hours, and document this in the ops runbook.
findings.finding: The GHA schedule re-enable step (reconciler pause) has no automated or guarded recovery path if the remediation run crashes, is interrupted, or the operator forgets to re-enable. The schedule could remain disabled indefinitely, silently halting all future reconciliation.
findings.location: Safety section — 'disable the `reconcile-bridge.yml` GHA schedule for the window (primary)' and 'Re-enable the schedule after.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion requiring that `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` and its `-h` variant are updated to reflect `--repair`, `--dry-run`, and `--limit`, and that the golden-file test passes.
findings.finding: The CLI golden file update ('update the `fsck --help` golden files (`tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` + the `-h` variant) for the new `--repair`/`--dry-run`/`--limit` flags') is described as a deliverable in Round-5 refinements but has no covering acceptance criterion — no AC requires that the golden files be updated and pass.
findings.location: Round-5 refinements (CLI GOLDEN bullet) vs Acceptance Criteria (all six AC lines)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: '- [ ] `fsck --help` and `fsck -h` golden files (`tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` and `-h` variant) are updated for `--repair`/`--dry-run`/`--limit`; golden-file CI check passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC5 to require a synthetic-fixture test that a human-triage-type ORPHAN_EVENT (e.g., STATUS) is NOT auto-rebuilt but is instead surfaced to triage, verifying the routing-by-type logic.
findings.finding: The body and Round-5 describe a critical routing decision for ORPHAN_EVENT — events of AUTO-RECOVER type are rebuilt, events of HUMAN-TRIAGE (LWW-scalar) type are NOT auto-rebuilt and are surfaced to triage — but no acceptance criterion explicitly tests that this routing-by-type behavior is correct (e.g., a synthetic fixture with a STATUS orphan should NOT be auto-rebuilt).
findings.location: Orphan disposition policy section + Round-5 (ORPHAN TRIAGE vs REBUILD bullet) vs AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC5 to: '... ORPHAN_EVENT repair correctly (additive-type orphan is rebuilt; LWW-scalar-type orphan is routed to human triage, NOT auto-rebuilt) ...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify git-push error handling: define whether a push failure aborts the batch run or retries (with backoff), log the failure explicitly, and verify the pre-a3-remediation tag is confirmed on the remote before proceeding with subsequent batches.
findings.finding: The plan specifies per-batch `git commit && git push` but does not define error-handling behavior when the push fails (network partition, ref conflict, remote rejection). A failed push leaves local commits un-pushed, diverging local and remote state, with no stated retry/backoff, abort policy, or operator alert — making the recovery path (rollback via remote tag) potentially unreachable.
findings.location: Safety section — 'git -C <tracker> add -A && git -C <tracker> commit -m ... && git -C <tracker> push' / AC: 'Live repair commits+pushes each batch'
findings.suggested_fix: After each `git push`, assert the remote ref matches the expected OID (e.g., via `git ls-remote`); on failure, log a clear error and abort the run (do not proceed to next batch), prompting the operator to resolve and re-run from the last clean fsck state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling policy for `git push` failure during a batch: e.g., retry N times with backoff, then halt the run and require operator intervention before continuing, with the local committed state preserved and documented.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens when `git push` fails during a per-batch commit. Since each batch ends with `git commit && git push`, a push failure (e.g., network error, rejected non-fast-forward) leaves committed-but-unpushed changes in the local repo, and the plan provides no retry, abort, or skip-ahead policy.
findings.location: Safety section — 'git -C <tracker> add -A && git -C <tracker> commit -m ... && git -C <tracker> push'
findings.suggested_fix: After `git push` fails after N retries, halt the remediation run (do not start the next batch), print the local commit hash and instruct the operator to resolve the push conflict (fetch + rebase or force-with-lease), then re-run with `--resume`. Document this in the ops runbook.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit synthetic-fixture test asserting that an ORPHAN_EVENT whose event type is a HUMAN-TRIAGE LWW-scalar (e.g. STATUS, EDIT) causes `fsck --repair` to produce zero snapshot writes and emit a triage log entry — NOT a rebuilt snapshot — to cover the negative routing branch.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that ORPHAN_EVENT repair is routed by event type — AUTO-RECOVER additive types are rebuilt, but HUMAN-TRIAGE LWW-scalar types must NOT be auto-rebuilt and must be surfaced for triage. This is a new behavioral branch with data-integrity consequences (wrong routing can silently corrupt scalar fields by replaying in an incorrect order), but the AC's synthetic-fixture test line only says 'ORPHAN_EVENT repair correctly' without explicitly requiring a test for the negative case: a LWW-scalar ORPHAN_EVENT must produce zero snapshot writes and a triage log entry, not a rebuild.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – 'Synthetic-fixture unit tests: known SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT / ORPHAN_EVENT / MISSING_CREATE (incl. .retired) repair correctly'; also 'Repair per fault class – ORPHAN_EVENT' and 'Orphan disposition policy'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC's synthetic-fixture bullet, enumerate both sub-cases: '(a) an AUTO-RECOVER additive orphan (e.g. COMMENT) is rebuilt and the snapshot is rewritten; (b) a HUMAN-TRIAGE LWW-scalar orphan (e.g. STATUS) produces zero writes and a triage record.' Add a corresponding fixture test for case (b) in the test plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require the store write-lock (`lock.acquire` / `lock.write_lock`) to be held for each per-ticket retire-rename + git-stage sequence in the `fsck --repair` SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT path, matching how compact.py serializes all file mutations under the same lock invariant (I5).
findings.finding: The SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT repair (retire-still-present) does not require holding the store write-lock during the file rename and git commit, creating a TOCTOU window where a concurrent compaction on the same ticket could interleave: compact reads source files, fsck --repair renames them to .retired, compact then attempts os.remove on the now-absent .json — the SNAPSHOT may be written referencing UUIDs whose files are neither .json nor .retired-consistently. The codebase write path (compact.py _compact_locked, event_append.write_and_push) always acquires the store write-lock before any file mutation; the plan's repair path must do the same.
findings.location: Repair per fault class — SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT; Safety section; Scope: `_commands/fsck.py` (`--repair` path)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `--repair` handler for SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT, wrap the retire-rename(s) and `git add` for each ticket inside `with lock.write_lock(tracker): ...` (the same pattern as `_compact_locked`). Since the reconciler is paused and batch size is 200 tickets, the per-ticket lock-acquire overhead is negligible. Document this in the Scope section alongside the existing atomic-write requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the orphan disposition policy table to explicitly classify all KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (add ARCHIVED, REVERT, BRIDGE_ALERT, WORKFLOW_RUN, WORKFLOW_STEP to either AUTO-RECOVER or HUMAN TRIAGE, with rationale), and add a catch-all fallback disposition for any KNOWN type not listed.
findings.finding: The orphan-routing decision table in 'Orphan disposition policy' is structurally incomplete: five event types present in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES — ARCHIVED, REVERT, BRIDGE_ALERT, WORKFLOW_RUN, WORKFLOW_STEP — are absent from both AUTO-RECOVER and HUMAN TRIAGE lists. The fsck --repair path 'routes by event type accordingly' but has no specified disposition for these types, leaving an executor with an undefined decision branch.
findings.location: Orphan disposition policy section; cross-referenced against src/rebar/reducer/_version.py KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (lines 65–94)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Orphan disposition policy': REVERT → HUMAN TRIAGE (order-sensitive undo semantics); ARCHIVED → HUMAN TRIAGE (LWW lifecycle state); BRIDGE_ALERT → AUTO-RECOVER (additive informational); WORKFLOW_RUN / WORKFLOW_STEP → HUMAN TRIAGE (ordered run-state). Add a catch-all: 'any KNOWN_EVENT_TYPE not listed above → HUMAN TRIAGE (safe default)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the `.a3-repaired` local marker is worth its implementation complexity for a one-time remediation script — given the plan's own statement that fsck re-check is authoritative, consider dropping the marker and relying solely on fsck re-scan for skip logic (simpler, zero new abstractions, same resumability guarantee).
findings.finding: The `.a3-repaired` local marker is a zero-prior-call-site abstraction whose own plan text declares it non-authoritative ('the AUTHORITATIVE resumability check is fsck itself'), making its necessity unclear and potentially over-engineering a one-time script.
findings.location: Safety section — 'A per-ticket `.a3-repaired` marker is a LOCAL, gitignored optimization only'; AC line 4 — 'a test verifies a re-run skips already-clean tickets'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `.a3-repaired` marker entirely; the skip-already-clean-tickets behaviour (AC 4) is already provided by re-running `fsck --repair` which only acts on tickets fsck still flags. The AC test can verify this directly: run repair twice on a fixture and assert the second run makes zero writes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC1's test to assert that dry-run stdout contains at least one per-ticket before/after diff entry for each fault class present in the fixture (e.g., `assert 'SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT' in captured_output`).
findings.finding: AC1 asserts that a test checks zero file writes and zero git commits in dry-run mode, but no assertion verifies that the dry-run actually PRINTS per-ticket before/after diffs and dispositions — the test could pass while emitting nothing, leaving the output contract untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria - AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the interface contract for A1's retire helper that A3 will call: function name, parameters, return value, and error behavior (e.g., raises vs. returns status for already-retired files).
findings.finding: The interface contract between fsck --repair and A1's retire-still-present helper is unspecified: the plan says 'calls A1's helper' but does not define the function signature, expected return value, or error-surface, leaving the coupling implicit and fragile if A1's implementation diverges.
findings.location: Scope section / Round-5 refinements: 'HELPER OWNERSHIP: the retire-still-present logic lives in the fsck --repair path (reusing A1's RETIRED_SUFFIX/rename); A1 ... owns all compact.py changes ... it calls A1's helper.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line contract to Scope or Round-5: e.g., 'A1 exposes `retire_source(path: Path) -> RetireResult` (RetireResult: {renamed_to, was_already_retired}); raises `FileNotFoundError` only if the source is absent and not already retired. A3 imports this from `rebar._store.compact` (or wherever A1 places it).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a code-level artifact (e.g., a frozenset `_AUTO_RECOVER_TYPES` constant in fsck.py or a shared util) that is the authoritative home for the AUTO-RECOVER / HUMAN TRIAGE classification, so the decision rule is co-located with the fsck --repair dispatch logic.
findings.finding: The orphan-routing decision rule (which event-type string maps to AUTO-RECOVER vs HUMAN TRIAGE) is co-located only in the plan's 'Orphan disposition policy' prose section, entirely disconnected from the fsck.py Scope and from any code-level enum/table. The processing protocol needed by the fsck --repair implementation is in a separate section from the code artifact that must implement it, creating an instruction-locality gap.
findings.location: Orphan disposition policy section vs Scope section (fsck.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Define `_AUTO_RECOVER_EVENT_TYPES: frozenset[str]` in `_commands/fsck.py` (imported from `reducer/_version.py` or defined locally) — the authoritative routing table for the orphan disposition decision; fsck --repair dispatches against this constant, not against inline conditionals.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/__init__.py` and `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` as callers of `fsck_cli` that must be checked for compatibility with the new `--repair`/`--dry-run`/`--limit` flags, and specify how `no_mutate=True` interacts with `--repair` (e.g., `--repair` must be rejected or silently skipped when `no_mutate=True`).
findings.finding: The external callers of `fsck_cli` outside `_commands/` — `src/rebar/__init__.py` (calls `fsck_cli([], repo_root=repo_root, no_mutate=report_only)`) and `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (calls `fsck_cli(rest)`) — are not enumerated as consumers in the Scope or AC. When `--repair` mode is added, the `no_mutate=True` path in `__init__.py` has no guard against accidentally accepting `--repair` from a library caller, and neither call site is listed for a compatibility check.
findings.location: Scope section; `src/rebar/__init__.py` line 1036; `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` line 260

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_snapshot_checks.py` to the Scope as a consumer to review/update when `--repair`/`--dry-run` flags are added to `fsck.py`.
findings.finding: `tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_snapshot_checks.py` (which tests SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT and ORPHAN_EVENT detection — the exact fault classes A3 repairs) is an existing test file outside `_commands/` that directly exercises `fsck.py` behavior. It is not enumerated in Scope or AC. Adding `--repair` mode that mutates repaired tickets' fsck output could break the byte-parity or output-format assertions in this file.
findings.location: Scope section; `tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_snapshot_checks.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a retire/cleanup path for `.a3-repaired` marker files: either delete them at the start of a fresh (non-resume) run, or document that they are cleaned up after a successful full run completes.
findings.finding: The `.a3-repaired` local marker files have no defined cleanup/retire path. The plan states they are gitignored and local-only, but never says when or how they are removed from the working tree. Over multiple partial runs or rollbacks they accumulate silently.
findings.location: Safety section — 'A per-ticket `.a3-repaired` marker is a LOCAL, gitignored optimization only'
findings.suggested_fix: At the start of `fsck --repair` when not resuming (i.e., no `--resume` flag or when `--dry-run` is used), glob and remove all `.a3-repaired` markers; alternatively, document in the ops runbook that `git clean -fdx --dry-run` should be used to identify and remove them after a confirmed successful remediation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC3 quiescence (e.g., `gh run list --workflow=reconcile-bridge.yml --status=in_progress | wc -l` asserts 0) and document it in the ops doc.
findings.finding: AC3 ('Reconciler paused by disabling the GHA schedule; verified no in-flight pass') has no concrete proving command. An executor cannot confirm quiescence without a specified check, making this step unverifiable in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria - AC3; Safety section ('Verify no pass is mid-flight before starting')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Verified by: `gh run list --workflow=reconcile-bridge.yml --status=in_progress --json databaseId | jq length` equals 0 before the first batch commit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC6 (e.g., `rebar fsck 2>&1 | tee remediation-report.txt; grep -c 'UNRESOLVED' remediation-report.txt` equals 0) and specify that before/after counts are committed to the PR description or a named ops doc.
findings.finding: AC6 states 'Live store reaches 0 UNRESOLVED faults; before/after counts recorded' but specifies neither a proving command (e.g., `rebar fsck | grep -c UNRESOLVED`) nor a location for the recorded counts (PR description, ops doc, a named file). 'Recorded' is unverifiable without a target artifact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria - AC6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the inter-batch wait configurable via a `--batch-wait` CLI flag (defaulting to 30s) so operators can tune it without editing source code.
findings.finding: The inter-batch wait of '>=30s' appears to be a hardcoded constant in the implementation rather than a configurable parameter, but it is a safety/performance threshold that operators may need to tune based on actual git-push latency or reconciler restart time.
findings.location: Safety section: 'Batched `--limit=200`, wait >=30s between.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--batch-wait=SECONDS` (default 30) alongside `--limit` in the CLI; document both in the help golden files.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
